text: Scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy and extraspondyric dissection with perineal extraction of the specimen for cancer of anorectal junction arising on the background of long-standing ulcerative colitis.

The surgeon performs a scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy with extraspondyric dissection, followed by perineal extraction of the specimen for anorectal junction cancer, arising from long-standing ulcerative colitis.
A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms.
A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms.
This found largely normal mucosa but a fibropithelial polyp at the anorectal junction.
The flexible sigmoidoscopy reveals largely normal mucosa with the presence of a fibropithelial polyp located at the anorectal junction.
No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy.
No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy.
Histology from the biopsy showed high-grade dysplasia with some features raising the possibility of deeper invasion.

There was no evidence of metastatic disease on staging CT.

The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient and uses laparoscopic ports in the left hypochondrium and left iliac fossa to mobilize the right colon, manipulating the tissue to facilitate exposure and dissection.
The surgeon stands on the left-hand side of the patient and uses the ports in the left hypochondrium and left iliac fossa to mobilize the right colon.

The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient, using ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa to approach the splenic flexure. The left colon and rectum are mobilized, with visible manipulation of these structures.

The surgeon performs a supracolic section to approach the splenic flexure, continuing the mobilization of the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs a supracolic dissection to approach the splenic flexure, using careful movements to safely mobilize the colon while avoiding iatrogenic splenic injury.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the colon, using precise movements to avoid iatrogenic injury to the spleen during the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing to isolate and manage this vessel as part of the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the laparoscopic portion of the colectomy by mobilizing the rectum, using instruments inserted through ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa.

The surgeon completes the operation by forming an ileostomy through the incision made for one of the laparoscopic ports in the right iliac fossa.

The postoperative course was unfortunately complicated by the formation of an infected hematoma in the right rectus abdominis superior to the ileostomy.

The patient recovered well, and follow-up CT at six months found no evidence of recurrent disease.

In summary, this case shows that in experienced hands, panproctocolectomy can be achieved laparoscopically with specimen extraction through natural orifices.
